examples.title: UDP Server Socket Creation
examples.description: Basic pseudocode for creating a UDP server that listens for incoming datagrams
examples.code:
UDP_SERVER()
BEGIN
 socket_fd = socket(AF_INET, SOCK_DGRAM, 0)
 IF socket_fd < 0 THEN
 PRINT "Socket creation failed"
 EXIT
 END IF
 
 server_address = create_address(PORT=8080, IP="0.0.0.0")
 bind_result = bind(socket_fd, server_address, sizeof(server_address))
 
 IF bind_result < 0 THEN
 PRINT "Bind failed"
 close(socket_fd)
 EXIT
 END IF
 
 PRINT "UDP Server listening on port 8080"
 
 WHILE TRUE DO
 buffer = receive_from(socket_fd, client_address)
 PRINT "Received: ", buffer
 PRINT "From client at: ", client_address
 END WHILE
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode demonstrates creating a UDP server socket. Note that unlike TCP, UDP server does not need to listen() or accept() connections - it simply binds to a port and receives datagrams directly.

examples.title: UDP Client Sending Datagram
examples.description: Basic pseudocode for a UDP client sending data to a server
examples.code:
UDP_CLIENT()
BEGIN
 socket_fd = socket(AF_INET, SOCK_DGRAM, 0)
 IF socket_fd < 0 THEN
 PRINT "Socket creation failed"
 EXIT
 END IF
 
 server_address = create_address(PORT=8080, IP="127.0.0.1")
 message = "Hello UDP Server"
 
 send_result = sendto(socket_fd, message, len(message), 
 0, server_address, sizeof(server_address))
 
 IF send_result < 0 THEN
 PRINT "Send failed"
 ELSE
 PRINT "Message sent successfully"
 END IF
 
 close(socket_fd)
END
examples.explanation: UDP client creates a socket and uses sendto() to send a datagram. Unlike TCP, there is no connect() call needed - the destination address is specified with each sendto() call.

examples.title: UDP Datagram Structure
examples.description: Shows how data is encapsulated from application layer to network layer
examples.code:
ENCAPSULATION(application_data)
BEGIN
 // Application Layer
 udp_header = create_udp_header(
 source_port = 5000,
 destination_port = 8080,
 length = 8 + length(application_data),
 checksum = calculate_checksum()
 )
 
 udp_datagram = udp_header + application_data
 PRINT "UDP Datagram: ", udp_datagram
 
 // Internet Layer (IP)
 ip_header = create_ip_header(
 source_ip = "192.168.1.10",
 destination_ip = "192.168.1.20",
 protocol = 17, // UDP protocol number
 total_length = 20 + length(udp_datagram)
 )
 
 ip_packet = ip_header + udp_datagram
 PRINT "IP Packet ready for transmission"
 
 RETURN ip_packet
END
examples.explanation: This demonstrates UDP encapsulation. Application data is wrapped with an 8-byte UDP header, then wrapped with an IP header for network transmission. UDP protocol number in IP header is 17.
